title: Making drum-carder batts from raw locks
subtitle: Preparing unwashed fleece locks on a drum carder — scour first, card into smooth batts
excerpt: Raw fleece must be scoured before carding. Soak in 60 °C water with dish soap, rinse at the same temperature, dry flat, then open locks by hand before feeding small amounts into the drum carder.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from drum-carding and fleece-preparation references including Clara Parkes 'The Knitter's Book of Wool' and general fibre preparation practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Hot-water washing of raw fleece to remove lanolin, dirt, and vegetable matter before carding or combing.
A flat sheet of carded fibre produced by a drum carder. Batts can be spun directly, torn into strips, or layered for blending.
Opening fibre locks by pulling them apart gently with the fingers before carding. Teasing breaks up clumps and removes large pieces of vegetable matter.
Hay, straw, seeds, and other plant material trapped in a raw fleece. Abbreviated VM. Heavy VM makes a fleece difficult to card and may require skirting or picking before scouring.
The smaller infeed roller on a drum carder. Fibre fed onto the licker-in is transferred to the main drum by the action of the opposing teeth.

content.text: Raw fleece arrives with lanolin (the natural grease of sheep wool), dirt, and often
vegetable matter
from the pasture. The drum carder's teeth cannot grip oily, clumped fibre effectively, so the fleece must be scoured before carding. Once clean and dry, the locks are opened by hand (
teasing
) and fed in small amounts onto the
licker-in
of the drum carder. The result is a flat, cohesive
batt
ready for spinning or blending. This technique is foundational to processing a whole fleece at home rather than purchasing commercial top.

attrs.title: Fibre dust
attrs.body: Carding throws fine fibre dust. Work in a ventilated room and damp-mop afterwards. Those with asthma should wear a dust mask.

items.name: Raw fleece locks (skirted, vegetable matter removed)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Scour the fleece

Fill a basin with water at 60 °C. Add 2 to 3 tablespoons of dish soap and stir briefly. Lower the locks into the water without agitating: sudden temperature change or agitation felts the fibre. Leave undisturbed for 20 minutes. Lift the locks straight out with both hands or a mesh strainer; do not squeeze or wring.
Scouring
removes the lanolin and most of the dirt in a single soak for most breeds. Heavily greasy fleeces such as Merino may need a second soak.
Step 2: Rinse at the same temperature

Prepare a second basin at 60 °C with no soap. Lower the locks into the rinse water and leave for 10 minutes. The key rule is temperature matching: moving fibre from hot water to cold water causes the scales to contract rapidly, which is the mechanism of felting. Drain and rinse once more if the water is still cloudy with lanolin.
Step 3: Dry flat

Spread the locks flat on a drying rack or mesh screen in a single layer. Do not pile them: the centre of a pile stays damp and may develop a musty odour. Leave in a ventilated spot away from direct sunlight, which can degrade the protein structure over time. The locks are ready to card when the tips feel completely dry and separate freely.
Step 4: Tease the locks

Hold a lock at the cut end and use the fingers of the other hand to tease apart the tip end, pulling the fibres gently apart into a loose cloud. Work through the lock from tip to cut end. Teasing removes remaining clumps of vegetable matter (which drop away as the fibres open) and loosens the lock so the drum carder can pick it up evenly.
Step 5: Charge the drum carder

Feed teased fibre onto the licker-in of the drum carder by holding it perpendicular to the roller (short direction). Use small amounts: overloading the drum causes the batt to clump and the teeth to jam. Turn the handle slowly and steadily, watching the fibre transfer from the licker-in to the main drum. Continue feeding until the main drum is covered to a depth of about 5 mm with an even layer of fibre.
Step 6: Remove the batt

Slide the batt lifter (or a blunt knitting needle) under the batt at one edge of the drum, running it across the full width. Once the edge lifts free, peel the batt off the drum in a single sheet. The batt can now be spun directly from the fold, torn lengthwise into strips for joining, or split into layers and stacked with contrasting fibre for colour blending.

items.cause: Agitation or temperature shock between wash and rinse
items.fix: Do not stir or squeeze the fibre in the basin. Match the rinse water temperature to the wash water within 5 °C.

items.cause: Locks were overfed or still slightly greasy
items.fix: Feed smaller amounts at a time. If the fleece feels waxy after scouring, run a second scour at 60 °C before carding.

items.problem: Batt tears when removed from the drum
items.cause: Main drum overloaded or batt fibres too tightly packed
items.fix: Remove batts more frequently, before the drum is fully loaded. Use the batt lifter across the full width to distribute the lift evenly.

items.problem: Vegetable matter persists in the batt
items.cause: Locks not sufficiently teased before carding
items.fix: Tease each lock more thoroughly by hand, pulling apart into a fine cloud. Pick out visible VM before feeding onto the licker-in.
